128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	
4	Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $k$ in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus $g = g(C)$.
5	
6	Because the Picard-scheme machinery (FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites) is blocked at the level of Mathlib infrastructure (see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A below for the detailed gap analysis), this chapter builds the Jacobian by an alternative ad-hoc route: the Albanese functor on smooth proper curves.
7	
8	\section{The Albanese construction}
9	
10	For a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$, the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from $C$. Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
11	
12	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
13	
14	Let $C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)$ be the $g$\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of $C$ (the quotient of $C^g$ by the action of the symmetric group $S_g$). It is a smooth proper scheme over $k$ of dimension $g$. Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
15	\[
16	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
17	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
18	\]
19	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing $P_0$ gives a translation isomorphism $\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}$, and the Stein factorisation of $\sigma$ yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
20	
21	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of $C$ inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
22	
23	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
24	
25	\begin{definition}\leanok
26	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
27	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
28	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
29	  Let $(C, P)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with a marked $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$. A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ is an \emph{Albanese object} for $(C, P)$ if there exists a morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ sending $P$ to the identity element $\eta \in J$, and such that every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ factors uniquely as $f = \iota \circ g$ for some morphism of group schemes $g \colon J \to A$.
30	\end{definition}
31	
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
35	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on $J$ --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on $\iota$ and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on $J$ sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on $\Jac(C)$ before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety $A$ in the universal-property clause.
36	\end{remark}
37	
38	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
39	
40	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term $h$ packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition $P \circ \iota = \eta_J$ and the universal-factorisation property of $\iota$. The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
41	
42	\begin{definition}
43	
44	\leanok
45	[The Albanese universal morphism]
46	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
47	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
48	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
49	  Given a pointed curve $(C, P)$ and an Albanese term $h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$, the \emph{universal morphism} of $h$ is the morphism
50	  \[
51	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
52	  \]
53	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of $h$.
54	\end{definition}
55	
56	\begin{lemma}
57	
58	\leanok
59	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
60	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
62	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
63	  The universal morphism $h.\mathtt{ofCurve}$ sends the marked point $P$ to the identity of $J$:
64	  \[
65	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
66	  \]
67	\end{lemma}
68	
69	\begin{lemma}
70	
71	\leanok
72	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
73	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
75	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
76	  For every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, there exists a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g$.
77	\end{lemma}
78	
79	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point $P \in C(k)$ as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that $P$, and applies the corresponding helper above.
80	
81	\begin{theorem}\leanok
82	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
83	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
84	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
85	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
86	  Let $J_1, J_2$ be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve $(C, P)$, with universal pointed morphisms $\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1$ and $\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2$ (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism $e \colon J_1 \to J_2$ satisfying $\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e$. The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation $\mathrm{id}$, and the produced morphism $e$ is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of $e$ as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
87	\end{theorem}
88	
89	\begin{remark}
90	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
91	  \uses{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}

103	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
104	
105	\begin{definition}\leanok
106	[Jacobian]
107	  \label{def:Jacobian}
108	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
109	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
110	  The \emph{Jacobian} of $C$ is the abelian variety $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ obtained as the Albanese object of $C$: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ of dimension $g(C)$ equipped with a universal morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$. Formally, $\Jac(C)$ is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-$P$) Albanese witness for $C$ provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
111	
112	  When $g(C) = 0$ the Jacobian collapses to $\Spec k$ itself, which is the terminal object in the category of $k$-schemes. When $g(C) > 0$ the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers $C$ itself (after choosing a $k$-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: $\Jac(C)$ is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-$0$ specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over $k$ of relative dimension $0$ is isomorphic to $\Spec k$.
113	\end{definition}

115	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
116	
117	The four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.

119	\begin{theorem}\leanok
120	[Group object structure]
121	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
122	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
123	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
124	  $\Jac(C)$ carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
125	\end{theorem}

127	\begin{proof}
128	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
129	  \leanok
130	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, $\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J$ as a $k$-scheme, where $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$ is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field $\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J$, so $\Jac(C)$ inherits it by projection.
131	
132	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if $\iota \colon C \to J$ is the universal morphism sending the marked point $P$ to the identity, then the diagonal $C \times C \to J \times J$, post-composed with the universal morphism out of $C \times C$ (factored through $J$ by Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
133	\end{proof}

135	\begin{theorem}\leanok
136	[Smoothness and dimension]
137	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
139	  \uses{def:Jacobian, def:genus}

141	\end{theorem}
142	
143	\begin{proof}
144	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, def:genus}
145	  \leanok
146	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$; the relative dimension $g(C)$ is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
147	
148	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of $C$ equals the genus $g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to $\Jac(C)$ at the identity is canonically identified with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of $\mathcal O_C$ is classified by an element of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$, and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$. Smoothness of $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any $k$-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of $\Jac(C)$ at every $k$-rational point is the same as at the identity.
149	\end{proof}

In the Picard-scheme route, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, which is itself proper over $\Spec k$ because $C$ is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for $\Pic$ of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ (and hence $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ after translation) follows from properness of $\Sym^g(C)$ together with the universal closedness of the surjection $\sigma$. Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, $\Jac(C)$ arises as the Stein factorisation of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ followed by a translation; $\Sym^g(C)$ is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of $C^g$, itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.

183	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
184	
185	\begin{itemize}
186	  \item $g = 0$: Then $\Jac(C) = \Spec k$, the trivial group scheme; $\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $0 = g$.
187	  \item $g = 1$: Then $\Jac(C) \cong C$ once a $k$-rational point on $C$ is fixed; $C$ is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension $1$).
188	  \item $g \ge 2$: $\Jac(C)$ is a non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to $C$ as a scheme.
189	\end{itemize}

191	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
192	
193	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.
194	
195	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
196	
197	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of $C$. This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	
201	\leanok
202	[Albanese witness for a curve]
203	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
205	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
206	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ is a bundle of the following seven fields:
207	  \begin{itemize}
208	    \item $J$: an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$ --- the candidate Albanese object.
209	    \item $\mathtt{grpObj}$: a group-object structure on $J$ in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
210	    \item $\mathtt{proper}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is proper.
211	    \item $\mathtt{smooth}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth.
212	    \item $\mathtt{geomIrred}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
213	    \item $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$. This refines $\mathtt{smooth}$ at the specific relative dimension $\genus\, C$ (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
214	    \item $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$: a proof that, for every $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
215	  \end{itemize}
216	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
217	\end{definition}
218	
219	\begin{remark}
220	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
221	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
222	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
223	  \[
224	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
225	  \]
226	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single $J$ is chosen once (intrinsic to $C$), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
227	  \[
228	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
229	  \]
230	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to $C$: a different choice of $P \in C(k)$ alters the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism of $J$, and so leaves $J$ unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-$P$ projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, and $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ each take a marked point $P$ as input and project the corresponding component of $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P$ via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
231	\end{remark}

240	[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]
241	  \label{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness}
243	  % Cycle-fix (iter-149 plan-agent): drop the spurious \uses{def:Jacobian}
244	  % from this statement-block. Mathematically `def:Jacobian` is the entity
245	  % that depends on this existence theorem (def:Jacobian's \uses{...} lists
246	  % thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness), not the other way around. Listing
247	  % def:Jacobian here as a hypothesis of the existence theorem introduced

250	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
251	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field $k$. There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ of relative dimension $g(C)$ such that, for every $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ is an Albanese object for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for $C$ (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
252	\end{theorem}

254	\begin{proof}
255	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:genusZeroWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
256	  \leanok
257	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-$0$ sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly over $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
258	
259	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a $P$-independent witness.}
260	  The witness $J$ depends only on $C$, not on the marked point $P$. Indeed, both classical constructions below produce $J$ as an intrinsic object of $C$ (the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$, or the Stein-factorisation target of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ translated by a fixed $P_0$); a different choice of $P$ in $C(k)$ alters $\iota_P$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism, and so does not change $J$ as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling $J$ as a single field and the universal-property data as $\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J$. We now turn to producing $J$.
261	
262	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
263	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
264	
265	  \begin{itemize}
266	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a $k$-scheme $T$, write $C_T := C \times_k T$ and let $\pi \colon C_T \to T$ be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
267	    \[
268	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
269	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
270	    \]
271	    A $T$-point is a line bundle on $C_T$ modulo a line bundle pulled back from $T$. The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C_T)$ descends to the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ and gives $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ a natural group-functor refinement.
272	
273	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For $C$ smooth proper geometrically connected over $k$ with a $k$-rational point, $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ (after \'etale sheafification, if $k$ is not algebraically closed) is representable by a $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k$, which is needed for the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ to be \'etale-locally injective.
274	
275	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ be the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$. As the identity component of a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is geometrically irreducible; for $C$ smooth proper of genus $g$, it is moreover a smooth proper $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$ is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the kernel of the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$, which is locally constant). Take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
276	
277	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each $P \in C(k)$, the Abel--Jacobi morphism
278	    \[
279	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
280	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
281	    \]
282	    sends $P$ to the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. It is universal among morphisms $f \colon C \to A$ to smooth proper geometrically irreducible $k$-group schemes $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$: this is the classical Albanese property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, proved by reducing to the case $k = \overline k$ (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on $C \times A$ trivialised along $\{P\} \times A$ correspond bijectively to morphisms $C \to A^{\vee} = A$ sending $P$ to $\eta_A$.
283	  \end{itemize}
284	
285	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
286	  \begin{itemize}
287	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product $C \times_k T$ and the descent of the abelian-group structure on $\Pic$ along $\pi^*$. The pieces are present individually but not assembled.
288	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$, which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels).
289	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction $G^0 \subseteq G$, and the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$.
290	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form.
291	  \end{itemize}
292	
293	  \paragraph{Route B --- Symmetric powers and Stein factorisation (historical alternative; not pursued).}
294	  \textbf{Iter-144 disposition.} Route B is a historical alternative \emph{not pursued} by the project. It is preserved here for scholarly context only. Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA --- is the committed M3 route per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition. The decomposition, gating Mathlib pieces, and midpoint LOC figure ($\sim 9000$ LOC) recorded below are the iter-123 audit values (\texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}); the chapter does not re-cost Route B under current Mathlib snapshots.
295	
296	  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.
297	
298	  \begin{itemize}
299	    \item[\textbf{(B.1)}] \emph{The $g$-fold symmetric power.} Form the product $C^g$ and let $S_g$ act by permutation of the factors. The categorical quotient $C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g$ exists as a $k$-scheme, smooth and proper over $k$ of dimension $g$. Smoothness follows because the action of $S_g$ on the smooth $k$-scheme $C^g$ is free outside the diagonal locus and the local-ring computation at the diagonal points produces a smooth ring (this is the symmetric-product computation of Fogarty / Mumford). Properness descends from properness of $C^g$ via the geometric-quotient property.
300	
301	    \item[\textbf{(B.2)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi morphism on symmetric powers.} Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
302	    \[
303	      \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
304	      \sum_{i=1}^g [Q_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g Q_i\bigr)
305	    \]
306	    is well-defined (the right-hand side is symmetric in the $Q_i$ and depends only on the unordered tuple) and is surjective. By Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, $\sigma$ is birational onto its image: a generic divisor of degree $g$ on $C$ has $h^0(\mathcal O_C(D)) = 1$, so the fibre $\sigma^{-1}([\mathcal O_C(D)])$ is a point at generic divisors; the special fibres are projective spaces $\mathbb P(h^0 - 1)$ over the locus where $h^0 > 1$.
307	
308	    \item[\textbf{(B.3)}] \emph{The Stein factorisation.} Because $\sigma$ is a proper surjection with geometrically connected fibres (each fibre $\mathbb P^{h^0-1}_k$ is geometrically irreducible), its Stein factorisation
309	    \[
310	      C^{(g)} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_*\;} \Spec_{\Pic^g_{C/k}}\bigl(\sigma_* \mathcal O_{C^{(g)}}\bigr) \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}
311	    \]
312	    presents the second arrow as a finite morphism whose source is the intermediate scheme. Since the fibres of $\sigma$ are connected, this intermediate scheme equals $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ scheme-theoretically (the Stein factorisation is trivial on the second arrow when fibres are connected, by Stein's classical theorem). The middle scheme is therefore $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ itself, which is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of dimension $g$. Translating by $-g P_0$ yields $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and we take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as in Route A. The universal property is then established as in Sub-step A.4.
313	  \end{itemize}

316	  \begin{itemize}
317	    \item Sub-step B.1 requires symmetric powers of schemes (the construction $\Sym^g$ for a $k$-scheme of finite type) and finite-group-scheme quotients of schemes (the quotient $C^g / S_g$). Mathlib has finite-group quotients of types and of various algebraic structures, but not yet a usable scheme-level quotient by a finite group action with the smooth-target property.
318	    \item Sub-step B.2 requires Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, including the relative version for families of curves --- the cohomology-and-base-change machinery for proper morphisms.
319	    \item Sub-step B.3 requires the Stein factorisation theorem for proper morphisms of locally Noetherian schemes ($f \colon X \to Y$ proper $\implies$ $f_* \mathcal O_X$ is a coherent $\mathcal O_Y$-algebra). Mathlib has partial coherence-theoretic infrastructure but does not yet have the cohomology of $\mathcal O$-modules of finite type along proper morphisms in the form needed.
320	  \end{itemize}
321	
322	  \paragraph{Genus-$0$ sub-case (absorbed into the same witness).}
323	  When $g(C) = 0$ the witness's underlying scheme is $J = \Spec k$, and the universal property reduces to a rigidity statement.
324	
325	  \begin{itemize}
326	    \item[\textbf{(C.1)}] \emph{Brauer--Severi identification.} A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ with $g(C) = 0$ and a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$. This is the Brauer--Severi triviality criterion: a Brauer--Severi variety of dimension $1$ is $\mathbb P^1$ over its base if and only if it has a rational point. The genus-$0$ identification on the curve side uses $h^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (Riemann--Roch) to produce a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_k$, which is then an isomorphism by degree-and-dimension.

332	      \[

337	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$ --- the unique $\bar k$-morphism factoring through the closed-point section $\eta_A \colon \Spec \bar k \hookrightarrow A$. 338	
339	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (the continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map is irreducible and closed) of dimension at most $1$. If $g = 0$ then $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous: the unique $\bar k$-morphism to $\Spec \bar k$ is the constant morphism. Henceforth assume $g \geq 1$, in which case the image is either a single point (the desired conclusion) or a one-dimensional irreducible proper closed subscheme of $A$ --- an irreducible rational curve in $A$.
340	
341	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by the following classical fact (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4, Proposition):
342	
343	      \begin{quote}
344	        \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
345	      \end{quote}
346	
347	      Two standard proofs are available; both unwind a few layers of upstream Mathlib infrastructure but are routine once the input lemmas are present.
348	      \begin{itemize}
349	        \item \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along $f$ induces a homomorphism of group schemes $f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$, where $A^{\vee}$ is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on $A$). The Picard group of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ has trivial degree-zero component, $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, because every line bundle on $\mathbb P^1$ of degree $0$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}$ (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence $f^{\vee} = 0$. By autoduality of abelian varieties ($A^{\vee\vee} = A$ canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of $f^{\vee}$ implies that $f$ itself is constant.
350	
351	        \item \emph{Via the cotangent bundle.} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
352	        \[
353	          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
354	        \]
355	        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism.
356	      \end{itemize}
357	      Either proof yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.

2867	G\x G$ is a closed subscheme.  Its $T$-points are just the pairs $(g,g)$

blueprint/src/chapters/Jacobian.tex

  directive's permitted `rmk:Alb` option (read it first; it covers the universal
  property exactly), so NO Serre/Lang no-quote flag was needed.

  `cor:sm` (dim $\le \dim H^1(\mathcal O_X)$, equality iff smooth at 0),
  `cor:ch0` (char-0 ⟹ smooth of that dimension), and the smoothness clause of
  `prp:P0` (smooth over reduced $S$).
- **Cited** `thm:Jacobian_proper` (`Jacobian.instIsProper`) — verbatim quote of
  `prp:P0` ($\mathrm{Pic}^0_{X/S}$ closed in $\mathrm{Pic}_{X/S}$ and proper over
  $S$ when fibres are complete).
- **Cited** `thm:Jacobian_geomIrred` (`Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible`) —
  verbatim quotes of `lem:agps` ($G^0$ open-closed, finite type, geometrically
  irreducible, commutes with field extension) and `prp:pic0` (specialisation to

  verbatim quote of `ex:jac` (generalized Jacobians form a smooth
  quasi-projective family of relative dimension $p_a$, projective iff $X/S$

  positive-genus match.

  Schemes" (§4 flattening stratification, §5 Quot construction) in the A.2
  Mathlib-status sub-step (the Quot/Hilbert representability engine for Route A).
